title: Will the UK's Defence Investment Plan finally be honest about Britain's defence?
gist: Drawing on new King's College London research, the author argues UK defence planning is structurally dishonest — spanning self-deception, budget 'alchemy' and self-interested lying — and sets out the concrete trade-off questions (strategic ambition vs. resources, sovereign vs. off-the-shelf procurement, global reach vs. homeland/Arctic focus) that will determine whether the delayed Defence Investment Plan breaks the pattern or repeats it, against a backdrop of a £28 billion budget shortfall.

insights.claim: New research by the author with Dr Maeve Ryan and Dr William Reynolds of King's College London finds that dishonesty is endemic across UK defence planning, operating on a spectrum from self-deception via optimism bias, to budgetary 'alchemy,' to outright lying for self-interest, and affecting every aspect of the process.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The central diagnostic framework of the piece: any assessment of the Defence Investment Plan should be checked against this three-tier dishonesty spectrum (self-deception, alchemy, self-interested lying).
insights.locator: A core problem of UK defence strategy

insights.claim: The Defence Investment Plan, originally scheduled for autumn 2025, remains delayed, and the key question is whether it will continue this pattern of dishonesty or break it by confronting the real choices and costs of UK defence.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Sets a clear pass/fail test for evaluating the DIP once released, rather than taking its framing at face value.
insights.locator: The issue is: will the DIP continue this trend

insights.claim: UK policymakers see Britain as a leading military actor despite a sharp decline in its capabilities, having explored ambitious projects including leading a coalition to stay in Afghanistan after the US withdrew, providing a Ukraine peacekeeping force, leading a Strait of Hormuz freedom-of-navigation mission, and contributing a Corps-sized force to NATO — each of which would individually stretch the armed forces to or beyond their limit.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence of a persistent mismatch between UK strategic ambition and real capacity — a pattern likely to recur unless the DIP explicitly scales ambition to resources.
insights.locator: In recent years, they have explored various ambitious projects

insights.claim: The first test of the Defence Investment Plan's honesty is whether it sets out a plan for defence that aligns actual resources with realistic and achievable strategic goals, given the 2025 Strategic Defence Review's ambitious call for the UK to play a leading NATO role and take on more European security responsibility.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Gives a specific, checkable criterion for judging the DIP against the 2025 Strategic Defence Review's stated ambitions once published.
insights.quote: does the DIP set out a plan for defence that aligns actual resources with realistic and achievable strategic goals?

insights.claim: At the outbreak of the 2026 US/Israel/Iran war, the UK had no maritime presence in the Gulf or the eastern Mediterranean and took weeks to deploy a single ship to reassure allies, and that ship then had to be diverted for maintenance.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A concrete, recent, embarrassing illustration that the UK's claimed global reach does not match its actual deployable capacity in a live crisis.
insights.locator: This was starkly illustrated at the outbreak

insights.claim: Decades of pursuing 'more with less' — using technology to offset declining force mass — have left the UK with a 'shadow force' spanning the full spectrum of capabilities but with so little depth in any one area that it has few real military options when a crisis breaks out.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Diagnoses why the UK looks capable on paper (broad capability spread) but is operationally weak — a framework for skepticism toward any future UK force-structure announcement that emphasizes breadth over depth.
insights.locator: For decades, policymakers have argued that Britain could do 'more with less'

insights.claim: The long-standing core assumption underlying UK defence planning — that the US will always take the lead militarily while the UK acts as a niche supporting provider — is no longer true, yet it continues to underpin UK procurement decisions.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Identifies an outdated but still-operative planning assumption that, if unaddressed by the DIP, would perpetuate strategically mismatched procurement even as US commitment to Europe declines.
insights.locator: the core assumption of all British defence planning for decades

insights.claim: Sourcing military equipment domestically to bespoke designs is slower and more expensive than buying on the open market, and the evidence for the economic benefits of domestic defence-industry spending is weak, meaning the DIP must make an explicit, costed choice between sovereign capability and off-the-shelf procurement.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Challenges the common political justification for domestic defence procurement (economic multiplier effects); relevant for assessing UK defence-industrial policy credibility and for defence contractors expecting guaranteed domestic-sourcing preference.
insights.locator: Sourcing military equipment domestically, to bespoke designs

insights.claim: If honest, the Defence Investment Plan will have to choose between focusing on defending the UK mainland and the Arctic/High North, or mounting a costly effort to reconstitute the ability to project force globally — the UK currently lacks the military resources to do both at scale.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Frames the single biggest strategic choice to watch for in the DIP's release: homeland/Arctic focus versus global force projection, since the UK cannot credibly fund both.
insights.locator: If it's honest, the DIP will finally have to make a choice

insights.claim: UK defence once again faces a financial black hole estimated at £28 billion.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A concrete fiscal figure to anchor how large the DIP's funding gap is and how credible any proposed savings or new spending commitments would need to be to close it.

insights.claim: The 2025 Strategic Defence Review and National Security Strategy were undermined from the outset by a promised £6 billion in efficiency savings that senior military officers, per the authors' research, privately acknowledged were either 'wildly optimistic' or put forward disingenuously for political reasons — an example of the 'alchemy' of pretending budget cuts would not affect frontline capabilities.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A specific, sourced example of the budget 'alchemy' pattern the piece warns the DIP could repeat — a concrete precedent to check the DIP's savings assumptions against.
insights.quote: wildly optimistic or put forward disingenuously for political reasons
insights.locator: The 2025 SDR and NSS were undermined at the outset
